title: Prompts and constrained outputs

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Prompts and constrained outputs; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile; July 2024; NIST AI 600-1 risk sections and suggested actions for confabulation, information integrity, security, privacy, and misuse; atomic topic: Prompts and constrained outputs
sources.claim: A prompt-plus-validator workflow defines task, authorized context, constraints, untrusted-data boundaries, and an output schema, then treats every generated field as untrusted until deterministic validation and policy checks pass.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A prompt-plus-validator workflow defines task, authorized context, constraints, untrusted-data boundaries, and an output schema, then treats every generated field as untrusted until deterministic validation and policy checks pass.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A model is asked for bounded JSON, the application parses exact fields, rejects extras and invalid ranges, and separately verifies cited identifiers. Your goal is to design and test a prompt-plus-validator workflow against normal and adversarial cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Convolution, sequence and attention roles; Natural-language processing; Tokens, next-token modeling and context; python io files json. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A prompt-plus-validator workflow defines task, authorized context, constraints, untrusted-data boundaries, and an output schema, then treats every generated field as untrusted until deterministic validation and policy checks pass. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Prompt wording cannot guarantee compliance, schema validity does not make content factual or safe, adversarial context can inject instructions, retries can add cost or inconsistency, and some decisions require human review. The learner must reject this overclaim: A detailed system prompt or JSON mode guarantees correct, safe, schema-compliant output without application validation.

includes: Specify task, context, constraints and output schema while treating generated output as untrusted.
Design and test a prompt-plus-validator workflow against normal and adversarial cases.
excludes: Prompt wording cannot guarantee compliance, schema validity does not make content factual or safe, adversarial context can inject instructions, retries can add cost or inconsistency, and some decisions require human review.

outcomes: Design and test a prompt-plus-validator workflow against normal and adversarial cases.

examples.title: Prompts and constrained outputs: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A model is asked for bounded JSON, the application parses exact fields, rejects extras and invalid ranges, and separately verifies cited identifiers.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A model is asked for bounded JSON, the application parses exact fields, rejects extras and invalid ranges, and separately verifies cited identifiers.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Separate instructions from untrusted context and validate syntax, schema, semantics, authorization, and citations outside the model before use.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Separate instructions from untrusted context and validate syntax, schema, semantics, authorization, and citations outside the model before use.

examples.title: Prompts and constrained outputs: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Retrieved text says to ignore prior rules; it remains quoted data and cannot alter tool permissions or output policy.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Retrieved text says to ignore prior rules; it remains quoted data and cannot alter tool permissions or output policy.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Prompt wording cannot guarantee compliance, schema validity does not make content factual or safe, adversarial context can inject instructions, retries can add cost or inconsistency, and some decisions require human review.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A detailed system prompt or JSON mode guarantees correct, safe, schema-compliant output without application validation.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and test a prompt-plus-validator workflow against normal and adversarial cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A detailed system prompt or JSON mode guarantees correct, safe, schema-compliant output without application validation.
misconceptions.correction: Separate instructions from untrusted context and validate syntax, schema, semantics, authorization, and citations outside the model before use.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A detailed system prompt or JSON mode guarantees correct, safe, schema-compliant output without application validation.

analogy.example: a bounded document desk that predicts tokens, validates structured drafts, retrieves cited passages, and exposes only authorized tools can introduce Prompts and constrained outputs only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: language-model context, output validation, RAG stages, and API/tool security
limitations: generation, retrieval, prompting, and provider trust remain fallible and cannot receive secrets or autonomous authority

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and test a prompt-plus-validator workflow against normal and adversarial cases. Keep this boundary in view: Prompt wording cannot guarantee compliance, schema validity does not make content factual or safe, adversarial context can inject instructions, retries can add cost or inconsistency, and some decisions require human review.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Prompts and constrained outputs with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
